title: Wise Mind Practice

Mindfulness Worksheet 3   (Mindfulness Handouts 3, 3a)
Wise Mind Practice
Due Date:             Name:                                    Week Starting:
Wise Mind Practice Exercise: Check off an exercise each time you do one.



	1.	 Attended to my breath coming in and out, letting my attention settle into my center.



	2.	 Imagined being a flake of stone on the lake.



	3.	 Imagined walking down an inner spiral stairs.



	4.	 Dropped into the pauses between inhaling and exhaling.



	5.	 Breathed “wise” in, “mind” out.



	6.	 Asked Wise Mind a question (breathing in) and listened for the answer (breathing out).



	7.	 Asked myself, “Is this Wise Mind?”



	8.	 Other (describe):



	9.	 Other (describe):
Describe the situation and how you practiced Wise Mind:


How effective was the practice in helping you become centered in your Wise Mind?
Not effective:
I couldn’t do the skill
for even 1 minute. I got
distracted or quit.
Somewhat effective:
I was able to practice Wise Mind
and became somewhat centered
in my Wise Mind.
Very effective:
I became centered in Wise
Mind, and was free to do
what needed to be done.
1                2                3                4                5
Describe the situation and how you practiced Wise Mind:


How effective was the practice in helping you become centered in your Wise Mind?
Not effective:
I couldn’t do the skill

Very effective:

what needed to be done.
1                2                3                4                5
List any and all wise things you did this week:

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Wise Mind Practice
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Mindfulness is allowed to be tiny, sensory-safe, and done from bed or wheelchair. Noticing without judging counts even when the day is messy.
tiny_steps: Find one neutral thing in the room.
Return to the next safe action.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Use eyes-open grounding if closing eyes feels unsafe.
Use quiet, low-scent, low-touch options for MCAS, trauma, and sensory overload.
No posture requirement; lying down or supported sitting is valid.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not force stillness when it increases pain, panic, dysphoria, or trauma activation.
